D7 Print L4 Analyze Reference

Contract Proof vs Press

a proof is an inkjet that pretends to be a press — its RIP bends its own tone curve until it lands on top of the press's known curve

A contract proof is an inkjet printer with a different native tone-value increase from the offset press it is supposed to simulate. The proof's job is not to look like the proofer's natural output — it is to match the press's TVI curve. The proof RIP precompensates so the measured proof curve sits on the press curve; any mismatch is the proof's calibration error.

The dashed curve is the press's published TVI target. The solid curve is the proof's TVI at the chosen calibration level — start uncalibrated and it sits off; slide calibration up and it snaps onto the press. The strip below shows the matching tone ramps.
